content:
4 min read
12 hours ago

--

I spent way too long evaluating templates early in my career. Tabs open. Demo sites running on three monitors. Feature comparison spreadsheets. Three hours to buy a $20 product.
Then I bought a bad one anyway because it had the nicest demo.
After building and selling templates since 2021 — and buying enough bad ones before that — here’s the actual checklist I use now. It takes five minutes and it’s eliminated almost every bad purchase.
The Package.json Test
Every template evaluation starts here. Not the demo. Not the feature list. The package.json.
What I’m looking for in 2026:
{
"@angular/core": "^22.0.0",
"react": "^19.0.0",
"next": "^16.0.0",
"bootstrap": "^5.3.0"
}What ends the evaluation immediately:
{
"jquery": "^3.6.0"
}jQuery in an Angular or React template means the developer used jQuery for UI behavior instead of the framework. That decision is baked into every component you’ll work in for months. The demo can look perfect. The code underneath is still wrong.
I’ve found jQuery in templates sold as “Angular 22 compatible” in 2026. The version number was updated. The code wasn’t.
Two Minutes on Lighthouse
Open the live demo. Chrome DevTools. Lighthouse. Run it.
A Bootstrap 5 landing page or admin dashboard with no real dynamic data should score above 75 on Performance. Above 85 if it’s a simple landing page.
Below 70 — ask the seller why before buying. Below 50 — don’t buy it. I tested a template last month with a Lighthouse score of 31. The seller said demos are always slower than production. They’re not. Not by 44 points.
The specific things that kill Lighthouse scores in templates:
Multiple icon libraries loaded simultaneously — FontAwesome, Bootstrap Icons, and Boxicons all on the same page. Pick one.
Unoptimized images — large JPEGs in the hero section that haven’t been converted to WebP. Should be flagged in the Lighthouse report.
Render-blocking scripts — JavaScript files loaded in <head> without defer or async. Basic stuff that should be caught before a template ships.
If a template seller didn’t fix a 31 Lighthouse score before listing it for sale — they didn’t care enough about the product. That attitude extends to support, updates, and documentation.
Mobile On a Real Phone
Not Chrome DevTools responsive mode. Your actual phone.
The specific things that break on real devices that DevTools misses:
Sidebar that opens but won’t close. The close button exists in HTML. The JavaScript event isn’t attached to it. Happens more often than it should. Tap outside the sidebar on a real phone and you’ll know immediately.
Tables that create horizontal scroll on the entire page instead of just the table. Fine on desktop. The entire page scrolls sideways on mobile. Broken.
Touch targets that are too small — buttons and nav items that require precise tapping. Modern browsers flag tap targets under 48px but templates often ignore this.
Five minutes on a real phone. If these things break in the demo — they’ll break in your production application too.
View Source for 10 Minutes
This is the check most developers skip and it’s the one that saves the most pain.
Open the live demo. View source or open DevTools. Look at the component files if accessible. You’re not auditing the entire codebase. You’re doing a smell test.
Good signs: folder structure with features separated from shared components, TypeScript files throughout, component files under 200 lines, SCSS with variables organized in one file.
Bad signs: everything in one app.component.ts file 3000 lines long, any type everywhere in TypeScript, inline styles mixed with Bootstrap classes, no folder structure — all files flat in one directory.
I reviewed a “premium Angular template” last year where a 2847-line AppComponent had jQuery inside ngOnInit. That was a $35 template. The demo was beautiful.
Last Updated Date
A template last updated in 2022 is technical debt you’re purchasing. Angular 22 has different patterns than Angular 14. Next.js 16 has async params requirements that Next.js 14 templates don’t implement. Bootstrap 5.3 has color mode support that Bootstrap 5.0 templates don’t use.
Check the changelog. Check the last commit date if there’s a public repo. Check whether the seller responds to support questions in the reviews section.
An actively maintained template absorbs framework update costs. You pull the update. An abandoned template hands you that cost indefinitely — every React or Angular major version means auditing custom components for compatibility yourself.
The $12 template updated quarterly beats the $49 template abandoned in 2022. Every time.
The Full Checklist
Before buying any admin dashboard template:

• Framework version current in package.json — Angular 22, React 19, Next.js 16

• Zero jQuery in dependencies

• Lighthouse performance above 70 on live demo

• Mobile tested on real device — sidebar, tables, navigation

• Source code passes the smell test — folder structure, component size, TypeScript

• Last updated within 6 months

• Seller responds to support questions
Five minutes on the first four. Done. Template passes all four — read the feature list. Fails any one — move on.
If you want templates that pass every item on this list — Angular 22, Bootstrap 5.3, React 19, zero jQuery, updated regularly — browse LettStart Design. Use FIRST30 for 30% off your first purchase.
